text:
Multi-Agent Systems with General Linear Dynamic Couplings: Consensus under Directed Information Graphs This note studies the distributed consensus problem for multi-agent systems with general linear dynamic couplings and directed information graphs. A major obstruction to current literature on multi-agent consensus is that, in order to achieve consensus, the global information of the communication graph, especially the smallest real part of the nonzero eigenvalues of the Laplacian matrix associated with the graph, has to be used to design the consensuality weights. The need for using global information precludes the consensus protocol from being truly distributed.

Existing results on distributed control and consensus protocols for multiagent systems typically assume global state information or full knowledge of other agents' states. In order to achieve a fully distributed implementation, in this paper, we develop a new adaptive consensus protocol that relies only on information on the agent dynamics and the relative state information between neighbouring agents. We show that leader-follower consensus can be achieved based on the new protocol as long as the communication graph contains a directed spanning tree rooting the leader. Monotonically increasing functions are included into the adaptive laws to handle possible asymmetry possibly presented in a directed graph. A new Lyapunov functions method is employed to prove the stability of the system, with the time-varying coupling weights being bounded by finite constants. The same approach is also generalized to deal with the containment control problems of multi-leader multiagent systems. Our designs always yield effective adaptive control protocols provided that the state controllability of the dynamics of individual agent is always ensured. This has been confirmed by several interesting simulation examples.
